title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Ethics of Human Gene Editing (CRISPR)

texts.A: The hard-won scientific ability to precisely edit the human genome represents a genuinely extraordinary medical breakthrough that carries the real and achievable potential to eliminate devastating hereditary diseases within a single generation of determined application. To turn our collective backs on this transformative technology on the basis of vague, speculative 'slippery slope' arguments and culturally specific discomfort about 'playing God' represents a profound and indefensible moral failure. [37]We bear an unambiguous ethical duty to alleviate preventable human suffering wherever we have the means to do so, and CRISPR represents the most precise, targeted, and controllable tool medical science has ever possessed for fulfilling that duty.[/37] While robust and enforceable regulation is clearly necessary to prevent its misuse for the creation of so-called 'designer babies' engineered for social advantage, we must not allow the speculative fear of a dystopian hypothetical future to prevent us from addressing the very real and present genetic suffering of millions of people alive today.
texts.B: The seemingly reassuring but dangerously thin institutional distinction between 'curing disease' and 'enhancing traits' is a boundary that will almost certainly and inevitably be crossed by those with sufficient financial means to access the technology through private clinics and permissive jurisdictions. History offers no examples of powerful technologies being successfully contained by mere regulatory aspiration when wealthy consumers desire them. [38]Gene editing, if left to market forces and national competitive pressures, carries a serious and demonstrable risk of creating an entirely new and permanent biological class divide in which affluent parents can routinely purchase heritable genetic advantages for their children, generating inequalities that compound across generations.[/38] I share the view that extreme caution is warranted, but [39]I believe a temporary regulatory 'pause,' however well-intentioned, is simply insufficient given the pace of development. We need binding international treaty obligations establishing a comprehensive moratorium on germline editing—heritable changes passed to future generations—until the full long-term ecological and social consequences of permanently altering the human species are genuinely understood.[/39]
texts.C: [38]I agree that the spectre of a 'genetic aristocracy' represents a genuinely significant and not merely theoretical risk, but I also share the view that the potential medical benefits of gene editing technology are simply too enormous, and the preventable suffering too great, to justify abandoning this line of research or accepting a permanent ban.[/38] The genuinely responsible solution is not prohibition but a carefully designed and actively enforced system of universal access that prevents the technology from becoming a private luxury for the privileged alone. [40]If gene editing therapy were deliberately structured and resourced as a universal public health good rather than a premium private service available only to the wealthy, it could theoretically and practically reduce fundamental inequality by systematically eliminating the arbitrary 'genetic lottery' that currently condemns the poor to disproportionately higher rates of certain preventable hereditary diseases.[/40] The governing principle must be equity of access and distribution, not simply technical safety in isolation.
texts.D: The public debate over CRISPR gene editing technology is persistently and frustratingly conducted without adequate attention to what I consider the most philosophically fundamental and ethically urgent issue at stake: the question of meaningful consent. [37]We are proposing to make permanent, irreversible changes to the entire future biological identity of individuals who have no voice, no agency, and absolutely no say in the profound decision being made on their behalf before they even exist.[/37] [39]I share the deep and well-founded skepticism regarding our collective ability to effectively 'regulate' this technology within the realities of a competitive global market; if one jurisdiction bans germline editing on ethical grounds, another will almost immediately seek competitive advantage by marketing the same procedure as an attractive 'medical tourism' destination to wealthy foreign clients.[/39] [40]I fundamentally disagree with those who optimistically present gene editing as a tool for social equality; it is, far more plausibly, the ultimate instrument for the total commodification of human biological life and the reduction of a child to a consumer product.[/40]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the primary ethical responsibility regarding gene editing?
questions.explanation: Expert A argues we have a 'duty to alleviate suffering' by using the technology. Expert D argues our duty is toward 'social inclusion' and respecting the 'consent' of those who cannot speak for themselves.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's concern about the creation of a new form of social inequality?
questions.explanation: Expert B fears a 'permanent biological class divide.' Expert C also recognizes the 'potential for a genetic aristocracy' as a significant risk.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the difficulty of enforcing international regulations?
questions.explanation: Expert D mentions 'medical tourism' and the futility of local bans. Expert B also calls for an 'international ban' because they believe a simple 'pause' is insufficient to handle the global pressure.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on whether gene editing can be used to promote social justice?
questions.explanation: Expert C believes it could 'reduce inequality' if treated as a public good. Expert D disagrees, seeing it as a tool for 'commodifying human life' and not a path to equality.
